sector: Industrials / Electrical Equipment (data-center connection, protection & liquid cooling)
bull_thesis: nVent has pulled off the cleanest growth shift in the electrical-products group. Its infrastructure business (the part that serves AI and data centers) went from about 12% of sales at the 2018 Pentair spin-off to 55% of sales in the first quarter of 2026, and the financial results are backing up the story rather than trailing it. First-quarter 2026 revenue was $1,242M, up 53% in total and up 34% organically (that is, real underlying growth, not just the optical lift from acquisitions). Within that, Systems Protection grew 76% total and 50% organic, and infrastructure organic growth topped 100%. Just one quarter into the year, management raised its full-year 2026 organic-growth guidance to 21-23% (from 10-13%) and its adjusted earnings per share to $4.45-4.55 (from $4.00-4.15), on the back of a first-quarter beat ($1.09 versus $0.94). Demand is locked in and reaches well into the future: a record $2.6B backlog (most of it stretching into 2027), organic orders up 40%, and a book-to-bill of 1.2x (orders coming in 1.2 times faster than the company can ship). nVent owns the hard-to-replace plumbing that sits inside the server rack: liquid-cooling distribution, high-density intelligent power distribution units, and cable management (about 80% of its data-center revenue), plus the enclosures and engineered buildings that house the gray-space gear (about 20%). It has deployed more than 2 GW of liquid cooling and won blue-chip design slots that become hard to switch out of once a customer specifies them: a Siemens/NVIDIA GB200 100-MW reference architecture and Google's Project Deschutes 5.0. New products added 20 points of growth against a roughly 3-point plan laid out on Investor Day, a six-fold over-delivery. Margins are widening even as the business ramps (Systems Protection return on sales up 220 basis points to 22.7%), the balance sheet is pristine (net leverage of 1.5x, effectively a net-cash position, a 5% dividend increase, and an ongoing buyback), and capital spending is up 40% (a new liquid-cooling plant in Blaine, Minnesota) — the company is putting its own money behind the durability claim. As the physical chokepoint that every dollar a hyperscaler spends on power and cooling has to flow through, nVent is well placed on the right side of the AI and electrification build-out, with customer roadmaps cited out through 2030.
bear_thesis: The quality is real, but the share price has already raced ahead of it, and the case offers almost nothing that can play out independently of the broader theme. At about $177, nVent trades at roughly 36.7x forward earnings and about 33x EV/EBITDA (enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) — a fortress valuation for a company whose pre-2024 identity was a GDP-plus cyclical industrial earning about 10% return on invested capital. Today 55% of sales, and essentially every dollar of incremental growth, rides on a single big-picture factor: whether AI-related capital spending holds up — the very same factor that drives the entire scale-out group. A scare about hyperscalers digesting their capital spending would hit nVent's valuation and its order book at the same moment; the 1.2x book-to-bill and orders up 40% leave open the question of whether this is a one-time wave being pulled forward. Free cash flow lags earnings badly: price to free cash flow of about 75x, with first-quarter free cash flow of only $54M against a net-income run-rate near $0.5B — the ramp is soaking up working capital and capital spending, and the 90-95% full-year conversion guide is unproven (the real test comes in the second half). Input-cost exposure is live: Electrical Connections return on sales fell 390 basis points on copper inflation, and the claim that pricing fully offsets it from the second quarter is a forward promise, layered on top of an roughly $80M tariff headwind in what management calls a highly fluid environment. On competition, nVent is a strong number two or three in liquid cooling, not the category leader: Eaton just closed a $9.5B Boyd Thermal acquisition to build an end-to-end grid-to-chip rival, and Vertiv has scale — the most valuable open lane is contested. Growth is geographically narrow (Americas up 65%, Asia-Pacific negative). If AI capital spending merely slows from hyper-growth to still-strong, a 37x valuation compresses hard well before the long-term story is ever disproven.
sop_bull: This is a whole-company estimate built on full-year 2026 earning power. The midpoint of the full-year 2026 adjusted earnings-per-share guide is $4.50. The bull case credits durable hyper-growth (22% organic, backlog into 2027, design-in switching costs) and applies a premium valuation that still sits below Vertiv's of 42x forward earnings (Vertiv trades at about 44x forward earnings and about 49x EV/EBITDA; nVent's cleaner balance sheet and faster organic raise justify getting close to Vertiv) -> $189 per share on earnings. As a cross-check on EV/EBITDA: full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA of about $1.15B; at 36x EV/EBITDA (a premium to the current 33x for the order and backlog momentum) gives about $41.4B in enterprise value plus about $0.1B of net cash = about $41.5B of equity, divided by 161.7M shares = about $257 per share on EBITDA. Blending the two (weighting the earnings anchor more heavily given the cyclicality) lands around $200-215 per share. Bull sum-of-the-parts (valuing the business on its full earning power) fair value is about $205 per share.
sop_bear: This is a whole-company estimate that de-rates toward the peer floor as AI capital spending normalizes: apply the Hubbell/Eaton group multiple of about 27x forward earnings (Hubbell about 26.6x, Eaton about 30x) to a haircut full-year 2026 adjusted earnings per share of $4.40 (reflecting input-cost and tariff slippage) = about $119 per share. The EV/EBITDA cross-check at 24x (the group level, stripping out the AI premium) on about $1.10B of EBITDA = about $26.4B enterprise value plus net cash, or about $26.5B, divided by 161.7M shares = about $164 per share — but a genuine capital-spending digestion de-rate would compress toward the earnings anchor. Bear fair value is about $120-135 per share.
sop_breakdown: The base case is a whole-company estimate (no separate sum-of-the-parts discount — both segments are higher-multiple, data-center-levered businesses, so the parts do not add up to more than the whole). Full-year 2026 adjusted earnings-per-share midpoint of $4.50 times 33x forward (a premium to the Eaton/Hubbell group at about 27-30x for nVent's stronger organic growth and record backlog, but a discount to Vertiv at about 44x for nVent's number-two or number-three cooling position and contested open lane) = about $149 per share on earnings. EV/EBITDA cross-check: about $1.13B of full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA times 30x (the midpoint of the current 33x and a group-normalized 24x) = about $33.9B enterprise value plus about $0.1B net cash = about $210 per share — richer because the EBITDA momentum is being capitalized. Blending the two, and weighting the earnings anchor for cyclicality discipline, lands around $165 per share. A few overlays: the effectively net-cash balance sheet (net leverage of 1.5x, net debt of about -$1.5B per stockanalysis) is a modest positive but not a special-situation driver; the 5% dividend increase and $50M-per-quarter buyback are shareholder returns, not a catalyst that forces a re-rating.
risk_reward: At about $177, the upside to the base-case fair value of about $165 is negative (about -7%); the stock is already trading slightly past a disciplined base-case estimate. The bull case of about $205 is +16%; the bear de-rate of about $120-135 is -24% to -32%. Downside to the bear case (about -28% at the midpoint) versus upside to the bull case (+16%) works out to roughly 1.8 to 1 against. The trade-off is unfavorable at today's price because the AI capital-spending optionality is largely baked into the valuation; the risk-reward only turns attractive on a meaningful pullback toward the high-$130s or low-$140s.
peer_comps_note: These are the true scale-out peers (data-center power and cooling, and electrical infrastructure), with live valuations verified in June 2026: Vertiv (VRT) at about 44x forward earnings and about 49x EV/EBITDA — the pure-play thermal-and-power leader, with the richest valuation; Eaton (ETN) at about 30x forward earnings — a diversified grid-to-chip giant, now armed with the $9.5B Boyd Thermal deal (March 2026) that lands directly in nVent's liquid-cooling lane; Hubbell (HUBB) at about 26.6x forward earnings and about 20x EV/EBITDA — a utility and electrical name with a lower data-center mix, the cheapest of the group. nVent (NVT) sits at about 36.7x forward earnings and about 33x EV/EBITDA — above Eaton and Hubbell, below Vertiv. Ranking the group: on growth, nVent is the leader (organic up 34%, full-year organic guide of 21-23% raised from 10-13%, beating the group); on margins, mid-pack (operating margin about 16%, gross about 37%; below Vertiv's structurally higher, software-like cooling margins, comparable to Eaton and Hubbell); on returns, the laggard (return on invested capital about 10%, return on equity about 13% — well below Eaton's high-teens and Hubbell's stronger returns; this is the key quality gap that keeps the moat narrow); on AI-capital-spending exposure, among the highest (infrastructure at 55% of sales, with nearly all incremental growth tied to data centers), more concentrated than the diversified Eaton and Hubbell, comparable to Vertiv. Net: nVent is priced as a growth leader (justified on its organic growth) but earns a laggard's return on invested capital — the valuation leans on the durability of the capital-spending wave, not on through-cycle returns.
role_in_scaleout: nVent is the inside-the-rack connection-and-protection layer plus the liquid-cooling-distribution layer: the physical plumbing — coolant distribution units, manifolds, high-density intelligent power distribution units, cable management, plus the gray-space enclosures and engineered buildings — that every high-density AI rack has to buy to move heat and power to the chip. Demand is highly durable as long as AI capital spending and the shift from air to liquid cooling continue (being specified into the Siemens/NVIDIA GB200 and Google Deschutes reference builds creates design-in switching costs), but this is a narrow number-two or number-three position in a lane now contested by a re-armed Eaton (Boyd Thermal) and Vertiv, and the demand moves in lockstep with the rest of the group on the same single factor.
consensus_delta: There is no hidden mispricing left to exploit on the long side — the market already has this right and has largely paid for it. The street rates nVent a Strong Buy with an average price target (about $188) only modestly above the roughly $177 price, and the stock has climbed from a roughly $69 52-week low to a roughly $179 high — the AI-data-center re-rating has already played out. Wall Street is not looking the wrong way here; if anything it is looking exactly the right way and has paid for it (36.7x forward earnings versus an Eaton/Hubbell group at 27-30x). The only genuine gap between expectations and reality is on the bear side: the consensus extrapolates 21-23% organic growth and prices the backlog into 2027 at a premium, quietly assuming AI capital spending holds up past the current hyperscaler wave and that full-year free-cash-flow conversion snaps to 90-95% after a first quarter of only $54M. Those are the assumptions most exposed to being wrong. The vulnerable street view is that this is a structural compounder that deserves a near-Vertiv valuation — when return on invested capital is only about 10%, the most valuable cooling lane is contested by Eaton and Vertiv, and the entire growth engine shares one big-picture driver (AI capital spending) with the rest of the group. This is a quality name with the gains already extracted, not a trade where the market is offside.
valley_of_death: Three near-term hazards in the risky window of the next 3-9 months. (1) Free-cash-flow disappointment: first-quarter free cash flow was only $54M against a roughly $0.5B net-income run-rate; the ramp is eating working capital and capital spending is up 40% to $130M. If second-half conversion does not visibly accelerate toward the 90-95% guide, the roughly 75x price-to-free-cash-flow gets challenged and the cash-compounder story cracks. (2) Margin slippage from copper and fuel inflation (about 1 point worse than prior guidance) and roughly $80M of tariff headwind in what management calls a highly fluid environment — Electrical Connections return on sales already fell 390 basis points; the claim that pricing fully offsets it from the second quarter is the next print's make-or-break. (3) Any headline about AI capital-spending digestion or slowing orders anywhere in the hyperscaler world would hit nVent's valuation and its order book at the same time — a 1.2x book-to-bill and orders up 40% set a high bar, so any sequential easing reads as a peak-orders signal. Stepping back to the bigger picture: AI capital spending is about 40% of 2026 S&P earnings growth and the single dominant factor moving the market right now — the tripwire most likely to break this name is the same one flagged for the whole book.
positioning_washout: There is little of the classic forced-selling dynamic here — nVent is not an income name (0.5% yield), so there is no income-focused holder base that would be forced to liquidate, and there is no business pivot or dividend cut in play. The relevant mechanical risk runs the other way: nVent has been a crowding magnet for momentum and AI-infrastructure money (up from about $69 to about $179) and is widely held by growth and thematic AI-capital-spending funds. The danger is a momentum unwind, not a value washout — if AI capital spending triggers de-grossing (funds cutting exposure across the board), crowded thematic names like nVent, Vertiv, and the rest of the cooling group sell off together mechanically, with no natural value buyer until a much lower valuation. There is no capitulation flush to buy into here yet; the shareholder base is positioned for offense, not trapped.
forcing_function: The next hard date is second-quarter 2026 earnings (roughly late July or August 2026), which will test (a) whether pricing fully offset copper and tariff inflation as promised, (b) whether free-cash-flow conversion turned up toward the 90-95% guide, and (c) whether organic orders held the 40% growth and 1.2x book-to-bill without a peak-and-fade. A clean second quarter would confirm the durability story but is already largely priced in; any wobble on free cash flow, Electrical Connections margins, or order momentum is the trigger that forces the valuation down. Eaton's grid-to-chip push (Boyd Thermal) ramping through 2026 is the slower-burning pressure on nVent's competitive position. There is no special-situation hard date (no spin-off, deal, proxy fight, or power-purchase / regulatory milestone) — this is an operating-cadence story, which is exactly why the catalyst has already fired rather than being a hard, contractual catalyst with a fixed payout still ahead.
moderator_conclusion: nVent is a genuinely high-quality, well-positioned beneficiary of the AI scale-out — the inside-the-rack liquid-cooling-and-power plumbing every high-density server rack has to buy — and the deep-dive analyst's strengthening read, with a credibility score of 4 out of 5, is well-earned: organic growth of 34%, a record $2.6B backlog, a large early full-year guidance raise, widening Systems Protection margins, and a pristine net-cash balance sheet. But the committee's job is risk-adjusted entry, and on that test nVent fails today. The core catalyst — the AI-data-center re-rating — has already fired: the stock ran from about $69 to about $179, trades at roughly 36.7x forward and about 33x EV/EBITDA (above the Eaton/Hubbell group, below Vertiv), and my base-case fair value of about $165 sits below the current roughly $177 price. The risk-reward is roughly 1.8 to 1 against (bull about $205 = +16% versus bear de-rate about $120-135 = about -28%). Reading the broader market backdrop, our framework deliberately penalizes catalysts that have already fired and already run, along with theses that lean on a rate or AI-capital-spending rescue, and it demands that a name be able to play out independently — nVent offers almost none of that, because its 55%-of-sales growth engine shares the single dominant big-picture factor (the durability of AI capital spending) with the entire scale-out group; it would add concentration, not diversification. Quality is not the question; price and independence are. The call is to pass for now, with low conviction (2). The value-only conviction is also 2: stripping out the catalyst, the fair value is below the market and return on invested capital is only about 10% on a roughly 37x valuation — there is no margin of safety on the numbers alone (a narrow but real moat does not by itself cap it below 3, but the absent margin of safety holds it at 2). The moat is narrow but real and widening (design-in switching costs and Systems Protection return on sales up 220 basis points, offset by roughly 10% return on invested capital and a contested liquid-cooling lane against Eaton and Vertiv). There is no secular threat — nVent is on the right side of the build-out; its risk is cyclical and valuation-driven, not a terminal erosion of the business. This is a watch-it-for-a-flush name: revisit toward the high-$130s or low-$140s, ideally on an AI-capital-spending de-grossing scare that pulls the whole cooling group down together.
interrogator_dossier:
# nVent Electric plc (NVT) — a deep dive on management and the setup

Scale-out group B (the grid bucket): connection and protection plus liquid cooling. Sourced from the web; the latest data is the first-quarter 2026 earnings call plus live June-2026 fundamentals.

## 1. How the story has evolved
This is the cleanest growth shift in the electrical-products group. At the 2018 Pentair spin-off, infrastructure (the data-center and grid bucket) was about 12% of sales; it is now 55% of first-quarter 2026 sales (up from about 45% in 2025). The story has concentrated onto the AI data-center build-out: management's language moved from data centers being a growth vector to multi-year customer roadmaps through 2030 with chip manufacturers. The company reorganized into Systems Protection (enclosures plus liquid cooling plus power distribution; $895M, up 76% total and 50% organic) and Electrical Connections ($347M, up 15% total and 8% organic). It reframed the business around inside-the-rack content (liquid cooling, intelligent power distribution units, cable management; about 80% of data-center revenue) versus the gray-space content (enclosures and engineered buildings; about 20%). New drivers introduced: a liquid-cooling platform (more than 2 GW deployed), the Siemens/NVIDIA GB200 reference architecture, Google Project Deschutes 5.0, and the Trachte and EPG acquisitions. De-emphasized: the residential and commercial tail, and Asia-Pacific (negative organic growth). The story expanded and got richer — and the financials backed it up.

## 2. Claims versus the financials
Revenue of $1,242M, up 53% total and 34% organic = corroborated (not just acquisitions). Infrastructure organic growth above 100% = corroborated by the mix shift. New products adding 20 points versus the roughly 3-point Investor-Day plan = a six-fold over-delivery. Adjusted operating income of $249M, up 53%, return on sales flat at 20%, Systems Protection return on sales of 22.7% (up 220 basis points) = corroborated, though Electrical Connections return on sales fell 390 basis points on copper. Record backlog of $2.6B (up low-double-digits quarter over quarter), book-to-bill of 1.2x, orders up 40% organic = corroborated, with firm dates and reaching well into the future. Net leverage of 1.5x (below the 2.0-2.5x target), net debt of about -$1.5B, a $50M buyback, and a 5% dividend increase = corroborated. On cash: first-quarter free cash flow of only $54M (up 21%) versus a roughly $0.5B net-income run-rate; price to free cash flow of about 75x; capital spending up 40% to $130M — the story is richer than the cash; working capital and capital spending are drawing it down, and the 90-95% conversion guide is unproven. Guidance raised full-year organic to 21-23% (from 10-13%) and adjusted earnings per share to $4.45-4.55 (from $4.00-4.15) — the numbers are running ahead of even the bull framing.

## 3. Tone and confidence
Rising emphatically. An 11-point organic-guidance raise plus a $0.45 earnings-per-share raise one quarter in, a 1.2x book-to-bill, and a record backlog all point to an offensive posture. Management talks of winning with hyperscalers, neoclouds, and multi-tenants, and of roadmaps through 2030. The cautious notes are tactical and honestly flagged: copper and fuel (about 1 point), highly fluid tariffs (about $80M absorbed), and Asia-Pacific softness.

## 4. Guidance credibility
High. A first-quarter beat ($1.09 versus $0.94; revenue $1.24B versus about $1.11B) plus an immediate, large full-year raise. New products at 20 points versus a 3-point plan show the company under-promised on its own roadmap. Headwinds were named and quantified, and held within guidance. There is no pattern of sandbagging and then missing. Earnings credibility is established; cash credibility is pending the second half.

## 5. Where analysts will press
(1) Durability and the risk of an order cliff: is orders up 40% a one-time wave being pulled forward? Management's answer (roadmaps to 2030, backlog into 2027) is responsive rather than evasive, but unprovable for the back half of the decade. (2) Margin sustainability under copper and tariffs — the 390-basis-point drop in Electrical Connections return on sales is the visible wound, and the promise that pricing offsets from the second quarter is a forward claim. (3) Competitive encroachment — Eaton's $9.5B Boyd Thermal deal (March 2026), a grid-to-chip rival, plus Vertiv's scale, were not directly addressed. (4) Valuation versus cyclicality (about 37x forward on a company with a GDP-plus history).

## 6. Red and green flags
Green: organic growth of 34% (not just acquisitions) plus a $2.6B backlog and 1.2x book-to-bill; new products adding 20 points versus a 3-point plan; net leverage of 1.5x plus a dividend and a buyback; Systems Protection return on sales up 220 basis points; the Siemens/NVIDIA and Google Deschutes wins plus more than 2 GW deployed. Red: free cash flow lagging earnings badly (price to free cash flow of about 75x; first-quarter free cash flow of $54M); Electrical Connections return on sales down 390 basis points on copper; concentration (infrastructure at 55%, with the marginal dollar all tied to AI capital spending); Eaton and Vertiv competing in the inside-the-rack lane (number two or three, not the leader); and a valuation of about 37x forward and about 33x EV/EBITDA.

## 7. Hidden signals
The company re-coded itself from thermal management and heat-tracing (a divested legacy) to liquid-cooling distribution and inside-the-rack content — curating its exposure toward the secular tailwind. Capital spending up 40% to $130M plus a new plant in Blaine, Minnesota show capital voting behind durability (and that is also what suppresses free cash flow). The new 80/20 split between inside-the-rack and gray-space content shows the company wants the market to model the higher-value inside-the-rack mix. The negative Asia-Pacific result is buried under Americas up 65% — this is a US-hyperscaler story, geographically concentrated.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict
The trajectory is strengthening — a genuine fundamental inflection corroborated by hard numbers, with disciplined, offensive capital allocation. The single most important thing to weigh: how much AI-capital-spending optionality is already in the roughly 37x valuation, and whether demand is durable past the current hyperscaler wave — the thesis and the 55%-of-sales engine share the same single big-picture factor (AI capital spending) as the whole group, leaving almost nothing that can play out independently. The moat is narrow but real (a genuine engineered-enclosure and design-in switching-cost franchise, but return on invested capital of about 10% only modestly above the cost of capital, gross margin of about 37% that is solid but not a fortress, and the most valuable cooling lane contested by Eaton and Vertiv), and it is widening at the margin (Systems Protection return on sales up 220 basis points, plus reference-architecture wins). The secular threat is none to manageable — nVent is a beneficiary, not a victim; the terminal risk is cyclical (capital-spending digestion or a shift in cooling architecture), not structural disintermediation.

Management credibility 4 out of 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening; narrow but real moat; moat trend widening; secular threat none.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact breaks this case. Every load-bearing figure checks out against primary sources, and the debate's own conclusion is already cautious (pass for now, low conviction of 2, fair value of $165 below the $177 price). The bear case — quality front-run, roughly 1.8 to 1 risk-reward against, free cash flow that badly lags earnings, single-factor AI-capital-spending concentration (infrastructure at 55% of sales), and a contested number-two or number-three liquid-cooling lane — survives the kill-check intact. The fair value of about $165 is anchored on the freshest data (the first-quarter 2026 full-year guide-raise midpoint adjusted earnings per share of $4.50), so there is no stale-anchor problem.
skeptic_corrections: (1) The free-cash-flow multiple is soft: the dossier cites price to free cash flow of about 75x, but first-quarter 2026 free cash flow of $53.8M annualizes to about $215M, which is about 133x on the first-quarter run-rate against the $28.6B market cap; the roughly 75x only holds if the unproven 90-95% full-year conversion guide lands in the second half — so about 75x is a forward, blended figure, not a trailing one, and the cash-quality risk is if anything understated, which reinforces rather than weakens the bear case. (2) Boyd Thermal closed March 12, 2026 (deal value $9.5B, about 22.5x 2026 estimated EBITDA, about $1.7B of Boyd revenue, about 90% liquid cooling) per Eaton's primary release — the competitive-encroachment fact is confirmed and materially real, landing directly in nVent's inside-the-rack lane. (3) The current price of $177.02 (close June 18, 2026), the 36.7x forward multiple, and the 161.7M share count all verify; the conclusion that the stock is slightly through fair value stands.
skeptic_evidence: nVent Q1-2026 Form 8-K press release (filed Apr 2026, SEC EDGAR CIK 0001720635): sales $1.24B +53% / +34% organic, adj EPS $1.09, FCF $53.8M, backlog $2.6B, FY guide RAISED to organic 21-23% and adj EPS $4.45-4.55 — every load-bearing figure corroborated. https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001720635/000162828026029098/q12026nvtpressrelease.htm

Eaton Corp 8-K / company release: completed $9.5B Boyd Thermal acquisition on Mar 12, 2026 (~22.5x 2026E EBITDA; Boyd ~$1.7B rev, ~90% liquid cooling) into Electrical Global, building grid-to-chip rival directly in nVent's liquid-cooling white space — moat-contest fact verified. https://www.eaton.com/us/en-us/company/news-insights/news-releases/2025/eaton-signs-agreement-to-acquire-boyd-thermal--expanding-solutio.html

Siemens press release (2026): Siemens + nVent joint reference architecture for 100-MW NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 liquid-cooled AI data centers; nVent confirmed in Google Project Deschutes 5.0 CDU (OCP spec) — both named design-in wins verified against primary sources. https://press.siemens.com/global/en/pressrelease/siemens-and-nvent-release-joint-reference-architecture-purpose-built-nvidia-ai-data

stockanalysis.com (Jun 18, 2026 close): NVT $177.02, market cap $28.63B, forward P/E 36.67, 161.72M shares; net leverage 1.5x / net cash ~-$1.48B per Q1-2026 10-Q — price, multiple, share count and balance-sheet facts confirmed. https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/nvt/

scale.role: Electrical enclosures / connection & protection for data centers and power.
scale.rationale: Real DC exposure (infra 55% of sales, named design-ins with Siemens/Google) but commoditized, broad-industrial, and a contested #2/#3 liquid-cooling lane now directly encroached by Eaton's $9.5B Boyd deal — a TACTICAL-to-low-LEVER franchise. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, corrections minor (FCF lags earnings even more than stated — reinforces, not breaks), ~8pt haircut off a ~52 franchise. Catalyst FIRED, base ~$165 ~7% below spot; second-order, wait.
scale.demand_durability: Medium-high — enclosures/protection bolted to DC build, but commoditized and broad-industrial.
